speaker: interviewer
text: Six. Interview with Eva Najnudel Kimel, January 23, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandmother, my mother's mother. Her name was Margolis Nyhaus Goldstein, when she was a young woman. I guess it was taken in Aradon. And I got this picture from a cousin in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These are my grandparents. My grandmother, my grandfather, whose names are Margolis Goldstein and Yerman Goldstein and my mother's youngest brother, Natanik in Krinitsa in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this picture, again, from my cousins in Canada. Excuse me, I want to change something. This is not the youngest brother, Natanik. This is-- this is Motek, who survived the war with us. And when he came to Poland in 1945 and was killed in a robbery attack in Wroclaw, 1945. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandfather, my father's father, who's name was Lazer Najnudel from Ostrowiec. I'm sorry, from Brody. It was a little town near Ostrowiec. He was a very pious and religious Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, again, I have no idea where that was taken, this picture. And I got it from a cousin in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think he was in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] He probably wasn't that old. If he was in his 50s, I guess, that would be it, but all the people with beards and whatever looked very old those days. That's all I know about this picture. I only got it because he send it or somebody from the family sent these pictures to Israel to my cousins. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, Fala Goldstein, then Najnudel, then Engelberg, when she was 18 years old. And, again, this picture was sent to her aunt in London, Ontario way before the war. And we got this picture from my aunt's daughter, a cousin, who gave it to me. I really appreciated it. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is, again, another picture of my mother, when Fala Goldstein, et cetera, when she was already in her early 20s. This was before she got married. And, again, I got this picture from the same cousin in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken in Aradon. Because my mother didn't travel much before those days. I mean, the only place she traveled was to Warsaw to have shoes made to her specifications. She had a good life, then. But then the war broke everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This, again, is a picture of my cousin Inca Jabner, who was only about two years older than myself. At home we used to spend many, many hours before the war playing. She, I learned, was a very bright and very talented girl. And during the German occupation, while she stayed in Aradon she was writing poetry in German. Unfortunately, she was killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken in Aradon too. And this was probably taken before the war because she was here a young girl. She must have been born in 1929, I-- I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, this picture I got from a cousin in Israel, who is a mutual cousin of ours. I promised I was going to make copies and send it back to her, but I never did. So I feel guilty, but she's sick anyhow. She wouldn't care, and her children wouldn't care, for sure. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this is my picture when I was, I think, about three years old with my father at our sawmill in Zawada. It's hard to believe that I was that age at one time. And, again, I got this picture from the same cousin in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Nathan. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the sawmill at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's exactly what it looked-- as a matter of fact, I used to build as a young child with other children that were the partner's children and some others. We used to build, like, tree houses there and houses out of, you know, parquets. And we used to have a lot of fun there. Lots of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself when I was, I think, about five years old. My mother, Fala, and my father, Nathan. And this picture, as far as I remember, as long as I remember, was made up into big portrait and was hanging in my parents' bedroom over their bed. I was really an eye in-- in their head. I mean, I was I-- I was everything to them at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture, again, I got from the same cousin in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture that's was taken in 1945. This picture, I remember when it was taken. And it was taken in Legnica. I am on the left, my sister Regina is on the right, and my mother, naturally, is in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it taken? What was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if there was any occasion for the pictures but, occasionally in Europe, people were taking portraits of the families. I guess they were much more fashionable than in today's day and age. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess we were all dressed up. I think from what it looks, we were probably, maybe skating or so. So maybe my mother bought us new outfits and she wanted to show her hat that was so fashionable at that time, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had this picture with you all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't have this picture because when I left Poland, I think I left a lot of pictures and everything behind. It's only when I went to, I think, my mother's funeral about 2 and 1/2 years ago, that I went through her closets, et cetera, and pulled out and took some pictures with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] as we know, my sister remained in Poland. And she's on the left. On the right is her husband, whom she married in Poland. And his name is Yanos Sikorski, and they have two children. Molgolsha who's in the middle and was named after my grandmother, Margolis. And her other younger daughter, who is on her left, her name is Ivana. I don't know after whom she was named, maybe after nobody. But-- Molgolsha probably doesn't know about it even that she was named after her grandmother. So, if she watches this tape, she will know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1917, and that was a picture that they send me. I guess after they took the portrait, they sent me to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you also give us your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Regina. And she passed away in 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that I received from my aunt from Valencia, France, where she still, I think, lives. Her name is-- she's in the middle of this picture-- her name is Fella Domanievitch Her husband on the right, Abram Domanievitch and their only son, Jean Marc, who lives in Lille, is married and has, I think, two children. Unfortunately, we are not in touch with them. Although, I send them a card but never received an answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your aunt from which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my mother's younger sister, whom I remember from before the war, who used to take me when she went on dates. To whom I really-- with whom I was very close at one time. And it's too bad that we don't know what's going on with them now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, this is a picture that, again, I found in my mother's apartment. And this picture was taken in Paris. I don't remember which year that was. On the left-hand side is my mother's brother, Henry Goldstein, who since passed away. Next to him is his wife [? Tolla, ?] who still lives in Paris and with whom we are sort of in touch. Next to them, it's my mother's first cousin, [? Heleg ?] Goldstein, who lives in Berlin and whom we visited this summer and stayed with, who is a wonderful, marvelous, generous, giving person. Next to them, it's my mother. And the two girls, I think one of them is my uncle's on the left, daughter Helen, and one of them is [? Carola, ?] who is this cousin [? Heleg's ?] daughter , but I don't know which is which. And [? Carola ?] lives in Luxembourg, married there, and lives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is a picture that was taken by a photographer in 1961 when I was on vacation in Poland. That was in my parents' apartment. I brought my son to show off. He was 10 months old when I went with him. And we were here observing his first birthday. Was a big party. I mean, big. Not big in people, but it was a big event. So on the left, that's me. And then the baby is, naturally, Martin Kimel, who was a year old at that time. It's my mother, my-- my stepfather, [? Micheslav ?] [? Engleberg ?], and my sister next to him, Regina, who was not married yet. It was a happy, happy occasion at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, this is a picture taken in July, 1990. And from the left, it's me, Eva, Alex, my husband, and my mother, Fala. This was some kind of occasion. I don't really remember who's, either bar mitzvah, wedding, whatever. As we see, everybody's here in wonderful moods and having a great time, especially my husband. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's also--

speaker: interviewee
text: And these are my two wonderful children, Pam on the left, naturally, and Martin on the right. And this picture was taken in July of 1990 at Uncle [? Sunya's ?] 80th birthday. The whole family was together, and we really had a wonderful time. Too bad these times don't come back. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to introduce your husband and tell us a little about how you met and what your experiences in this [LAUGHTER] community have been.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, my husband's name is Alex, or Alexander, Kimel. And we met in Wroclaw. A mutual friend brought him over one day while I was living in Wroclaw and-- and introduced us. They came to invite me and my roommate to a New Year's Eve party. Well, I did not go to the New Year's-- New Year's Eve party at the time, because I was going to Warsaw. I was-- I had a-- I was going out with somebody at that time. But I guess whatever it's meant, it's meant. So, a few months later, he called and made a date to go to see a show. Well I, in order to be presentable, had to go to-- to a hairdresser. And the hairdresser took too long to make me up, or whatever. I grabbed a taxi, and I was 15 minutes late. And I came to the theater and there was no Alex in sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I was very, you know, disappointed, because I was rushing [LAUGHS] to the taxi and went home. And I guess a few weeks later, he called again. He didn't give up. And at that time, we did go out and I still remember the show we saw because we went to see a show. What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the show?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, It's a funny show. I mean, I remember a little bit about it, but not much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're both from the same, similar parts of Poland, although no-- actually, you're from-- more from Galitsin. [INTERPOSING VOICES] How have your experiences brought you together? Your war experiences. You can talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it seems to me that what brought us together that you are minority of Jews. There were very few Jews in Poland. During the first years-- years of marriage, we never talked about the Holocaust. It was like taboo. It was too painful. Also, we were in Poland to come out and say that we are Jewish and so forth was no such acceptable, I don't think. And these are the reasons we really did not talk about the Holocaust until maybe about 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you wish to add anything more to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way have the last 10 years changed your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the story didn't change. The stories remain the same. The only thing that changed is the publicity, I think, about the Holocaust and Spielberg's movie and everything. That has made it very, you know, fashionable and brought it to the open. And so everybody talks about it now, and everybody wants to be remembered. And so the future generation would know where they came from and what were the parents' experiences and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that the problem that we had this-- in the first year, we had to establish our existence economic base. And this took quite a long time. So, being preoccupied with a career and raising of the children did not leave any time to go back to the past. In the last year, you know, we are much more established, so to speak and looking forwards towards, you know, the other part of journey through life. So you go back to the roots and try to remember. And try to pass on the heritage to children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to thank you for sharing your thoughts, your experiences, your life with the Shoah Foundation and wish you well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for coming, taking the time, and was nice. Thank you.
